currency.name: Maldivian Rufiyaa
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: 200 MVR (US dollars are accepted everywhere too)

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is the Maldives on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia (technically). The Maldives is a string of 1,192 tiny coral islands south-west of India and Sri Lanka, in the middle of the Indian Ocean 🌊

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of the Maldives?
mapQuestions.answer: Malé, a tiny island only 2 square km in size, packed with over 200,000 people. One of the most densely populated capitals on Earth.

mapQuestions.question: What language does the Maldives speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Dhivehi, the only language in the world descended from a mix of Sanskrit, Sinhala and Arabic. Most Maldivians who work in tourism also speak English well.

mapQuestions.question: How high above the sea is the Maldives?
mapQuestions.answer: On average just 1.5 metres above sea level. The Maldives is the LOWEST-LYING country on Earth, and one of the most threatened by rising seas from climate change.

mapQuestions.question: How many of the islands are inhabited?
mapQuestions.answer: About 200 out of 1,192. Around 150 more are dedicated tourist resorts (usually one resort per island). The rest are uninhabited.

foods.name: Garudhiya
foods.description: A clear, light fish broth (made with skipjack tuna), eaten with rice, lime and chilli. The traditional Maldivian everyday meal.

foods.name: Mas huni
foods.description: Shredded smoked tuna mixed with grated coconut, chopped onion, chilli and lime. Eaten for breakfast with hot flatbread ("roshi").

foods.name: Bajiya
foods.description: Pastry triangles filled with tuna, onion and coconut, deep-fried until crispy. The classic Maldivian short eats served with afternoon tea.

foods.name: Riha (fish curry)
foods.description: Maldivian fish curry, made with fresh tuna, coconut milk, curry leaves and screwpine. Eaten with steaming rice.

foods.name: Roshi
foods.description: A thin unleavened wheat flatbread cooked on a hot pan, similar to Indian chapati. Eaten with mas huni or curry.

foods.name: Foni boakibaa
foods.description: A sweet pudding-cake made with semolina, coconut, sugar and cardamom. The Maldivian celebration sweet.

scavengerItems.label: A tiny ring-shaped coral island ("atoll") with sand the colour of icing sugar
A water-villa on stilts over a turquoise lagoon
A clownfish hiding in a sea anemone
A green sea turtle gliding above a reef
A reef shark (whitetip, blacktip, or nurse) on a snorkel
Coconut palms leaning over a beach in classic postcard style
A "dhoni" (traditional wooden Maldivian boat with a curved bow)
A sunset over the Indian Ocean with not a thing in sight
A pod of spinner dolphins racing alongside a boat
Bioluminescent plankton glowing blue in the sand at night ("Sea of Stars")
A manta ray or whale shark on a Baa Atoll snorkel trip
Hermit crabs scuttling across the beach at dusk

geoMatches.description: The tiny but densely packed capital. Colourful buildings, narrow streets, a fish market, and the Old Friday Mosque (one of the oldest mosques in the Maldives, made of carved coral stone).
The atoll closest to the airport, with many of the best-known resort islands and dive sites.
A UNESCO biosphere reserve, famous for plankton-rich bays at Hanifaru where dozens of manta rays and whale sharks gather (May-November).
One of the biggest "local" islands (not a resort), where ordinary Maldivians live. Cheap guesthouses, public beach, and snorkel boats. The Maldives for budget travellers.
A small island famous for its glowing bioluminescent plankton, which makes the surf SPARKLE blue at night ("the Sea of Stars").
The southernmost atoll, near the equator. Famous for British WWII airfield remains, manta rays, and a more local feel.

senses.smell: Salt and coconut oil on Maldivian beaches, smoked tuna at a fish market, frangipani blossoms in the evening, the steam of fresh roshi being made, hot tropical rain on the sand…
senses.hear: Waves on coral, "Marhaba!" greetings on arrival, the soft slap of a dhoni boat hull, prayer calls from a small Maldivian mosque at sunset, parrotfish chewing coral while you snorkel…
senses.taste: Smoky tuna in mas huni, sweet fresh coconut, sharp lime on fish broth, hot fried bajiya pastries, salty Indian Ocean water on your lips…
senses.feel: Warm Indian Ocean water (29°C all year), powder-soft white sand, hot tropical sun, the gentle bob of a dhoni boat, coral underfoot when snorkelling (only on dead coral, please)…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How are the Maldives even there?
stories.body: The Maldives are 1,192 little coral islands sitting on top of an underwater volcanic mountain ridge. Millions of years ago, volcanoes rose up from the seabed; corals grew on their slopes; the volcanoes eventually sank back down again; but the coral kept growing upwards as the volcano sank, building rings ("atolls") that float at the surface. The whole country is the tops of these old undersea mountains, capped in coral. Some atolls are 80 km across (Huvadhoo, in the south, is one of the biggest). And every Maldivian island, big or small, is mostly just compacted coral sand and broken-up shells, growing slowly higher as new sand washes up.

stories.question: Why is the Maldives so threatened by climate change?
stories.body: The Maldives is the LOWEST country on Earth: an average of just 1.5 metres above the sea, with the highest point only 2.4 metres above. Sea level rise from climate change is a direct existential threat: even a small rise floods large parts of the country. The Maldivian government has been one of the loudest voices in the world arguing for action on climate change. In 2009, the cabinet held a meeting UNDERWATER in scuba gear to draw attention. The country is now investing in building higher artificial islands, like Hulhumalé (3 m above sea level), as long-term insurance for its people.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A whole country might have to physically move or rebuild itself higher because of sea-level rise. What would your country do in that situation?

stories.question: Why are Maldivian reefs so amazing?
stories.body: The Maldives sits at the heart of the Indian Ocean's "coral triangle", with warm clean water and minimal pollution. About 250 species of hard coral grow here, building reefs that house over 2,000 species of fish, plus turtles, octopus, mantas, whale sharks and sharks. Most resort and local-island guesthouses have a "house reef" right offshore that you can swim out to in 5 minutes. Some coral has bleached and died from rising sea temperatures in recent years (especially 2016 and 2019), and conservation projects are now actively replanting baby coral on damaged reefs. Going snorkelling here is a window onto something both beautiful and fragile.

stories.question: What is the "Sea of Stars"?
stories.body: On dark nights on some Maldivian beaches (especially Vaadhoo Island in Raa Atoll), the surf GLOWS bright electric blue. This is "bioluminescence", produced by tiny plankton (specifically a kind of dinoflagellate) that flash a blue light when they are disturbed by waves or footprints. It is a defence mechanism: the flash supposedly startles predators. Walking on a glowing beach feels like walking through stars. Best seen in late summer and on moonless nights. Not all beaches have it, and timing is hit-or-miss.

stories.question: How does island life work here?
stories.body: For most Maldivians, life is on a "local island", a small inhabited island with maybe 500 to 5,000 people, a mosque, a school, a few shops, a fish market and a small harbour. Fishing is the traditional livelihood, mostly tuna, mostly using the sustainable "pole and line" method (catching one fish at a time on a barbless hook, no nets, no bycatch). About 27,000 Maldivians make their living this way. Tourism is the country's biggest industry, and most resort staff come from local islands. The Maldives is a Muslim country, and on local islands women dress modestly and alcohol is not served (resorts are usually separate self-contained islands with different rules).

convoQuestions.question: Reef, beach, local island, capital, which has felt the most "Maldives"?
What was your favourite thing you saw while snorkelling?
A country at 1.5 m above sea level: how would you feel about that long-term?
How would daily life change if you grew up on a single small island instead of a big country?

wordSearch.hint: Maldivian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Green sea turtle
animals.description: Glides over coral reefs across the country. Often seen on snorkels and dives.

animals.name: Clownfish
animals.description: Bright orange-and-white reef fish hiding in sea anemones across the country.

animals.name: Whitetip reef shark
animals.description: Common on reefs, mostly harmless, with a distinctive white-tipped dorsal fin.

animals.name: Manta ray
animals.description: Huge graceful filter-feeders gathering at Hanifaru Bay, Baa Atoll (May to November).

animals.name: Whale shark
animals.description: The biggest fish on Earth. Snorkel alongside one off South Ari Atoll (year-round) or Hanifaru in monsoon season.

animals.name: Reef octopus
animals.description: Often spotted at dawn or dusk on reefs, changing colour as it slips between coral heads.

animals.name: Flying fox (Indian flying fox)
animals.description: The biggest bat species in the world lives in the Maldives. Wingspans up to 1.5 m. Often seen at dusk.
